title: Tomato Cage Planter
summary: A cedar planter box with a built-in wooden tomato cage rising off its corners — two fence pickets, three furring strips, one afternoon.
description:
A tomato plant needs two things a pot doesn't give it: root space and something to climb. This planter builds both as one piece — a fence-picket box with 40" 1x2 uprights at the corners, laddered with horizontal rungs, so the cage can't blow over or heel sideways under a loaded August plant the way a wire cone stuck in dirt does. The cage is anchored to the box; the box is ballasted by the soil. Neither goes anywhere.

The materials list is deliberately minimal — two 6-foot cedar pickets and three 8-foot 1x2 furring strips — and the cut list includes exact cutting diagrams for getting every piece out of those five boards with almost nothing left over. Follow the board-by-board layout; freelancing the cuts is how you end up one 15-1/2" rung short.

Cedar for the box because it's touching wet soil all season, and exterior star-drive screws because furring strips will eat the heads off cheap phillips screws. Line the box with a pot or landscape fabric — cedar tolerates soil contact, but drainage is what actually decides whether the box lasts five years or fifteen.

tools.note: Every cut is a straight crosscut — a miter saw does it faster if you have one.

materials.name: Cedar fence picket, 5-1/2" x 72"
materials.note: All eight box sides come from these two pickets — see the cutting layout in the cut list.

materials.name: Exterior screws, #8 x 1-5/8", self-tapping, star drive
Landscape fabric or plastic liner pot
materials.note: Line the box or drop a pot in — bare soil against the pickets shortens their life.

cutList.note: Cut each picket: 18 | 18 | 17 | 17. Pickets are nominally 5/8" thick.
The third 1x2 yields six; the other two come from the upright boards' offcuts.

steps.title: Cut all the parts
steps.body: Cut four box sides, long at 18" and four box sides, short at 17" from the Cedar fence picket. Cut four cage uprights at 40" and eight cage rungs at 15-1/2" from the 1x2 furring. Allowing about 1/8" for each saw cut, that comes to three 8 ft 1x2 furring. Square every cut and label each piece as it comes off the saw.

steps.title: Build two picket-and-upright panels
steps.body:
Attach two 17" pickets across two 40" uprights, two screws per joint — one picket at the bottom of the uprights, one above it. Build two identical panels.

Check each panel is square before the second screws go in: the picket-to-picket distance at the top of the uprights should also be 17", and the bottom edges should line up. These two panels set the geometry of everything after.
materials: Exterior screws, #8 x 1-5/8", self-tapping, star drive

steps.title: Join the panels with the long sides
steps.body: Stand the two panels up and screw the 18" pickets across the outside, into the uprights, forming the box. The longer pickets overlap the ends of the shorter ones — that overlap is what hides the end grain on two faces.
materials: Exterior screws, #8 x 1-5/8", self-tapping, star drive

steps.title: Add the first rungs
steps.body: Screw the first set of 15-1/2" rungs between the uprights on the open sides, fastening into the insides of the uprights. These tie the four corners into a cage instead of four sticks.
materials: Exterior screws, #8 x 1-5/8", self-tapping, star drive

steps.title: Finish the cage and line the box
steps.body:
Add the remaining rungs, spacing them so a hand with a tomato in it fits between levels — the cage is for the plant, but you're the one reaching through it all summer.

Drop a plastic pot inside or staple landscape fabric to line the box, add soil and a seedling, and park it where the plant gets sun. The cage will disappear under foliage by July, which is the whole idea.
materials: Landscape fabric or plastic liner pot
Exterior screws, #8 x 1-5/8", self-tapping, star drive

images.alt: Cedar planter box with built-in wooden tomato cage of vertical slats and horizontal rungs
